coredata.dc:title: Solvatochromism of high-efficient and long-wavelength emissive carbon dots for wide-range water-sensing in organic solvents
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Jiaqi
Zhou, Weiying
Chen, Jian
Liu, Ruiyuan
Pang, Qi
Zhou, Liya
Wu, Zhan-chao
Zhang, Xinguo
coredata.dc:description: A novel solvent-dependent fluorescent CDs (R-CDs) were prepared by solvothermal method from neutral red and urea precursors. The multicolor emissions ranging from blue to red from R-CDs could be achieved by tuning the solvents polarity. The highest quantum yield of 61.11% is achieved in R-CDs acetone solution with bright yellow emission at 565 nm. The solvent-dependent fluorescence is ascribed to the variation of CDs’ band-gap and surface potential, which are induced by polarity. R-CDs were used as a quantitative fluorescent sensor for detecting water in different solvents. An excellent linear relationship between water content (0–90%) and emission intensity is found in ethanol, methanol, acetonitrile, isopropanol, DMSO, and acetone. The limit of detection (LOD) is 1.55%, the recoveries range from 95% to 110%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) are 0.15%–5.43%. All results indicate that our R-CDs for water sensing exhibit good linear relationship and accuracy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Luminescence
Solvatochromism
Water detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) (B) (C) TEM images and the particle size distribution (insert) of R-CD. (D) FT-IR of R-CDs. (E) The wide scan XPS spectrum of R-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (F) Cl 1s, (G) C 1s, (H) N 1s and (I) O 1s.

(A) UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectrum of R-CDs ethanediol solution (Inset: the optical images of R-CDs solution under daylight and 470 nm excitation wavelength). (B) Emission spectra of the R-CDs ethanediol solution excited at different excitation wavelength. (C) Emission intensity of R-CDs at different pH value. (D) Emission intensity of R-CDs with different metal ions.

(A) Normalized emission spectra of R-CDs with different solvents under the 470 nm excitation wavelength. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of R-CDs in various solvents. (C) Emission wavelength as a function of the ENT. (D) Possible energy level diagram and polychromatic emission process diagram of R-CDs in different solvents. (E) The corresponding fluorescence photographs at 470 nm excitation wavelength.

Excitation-dependent PL spectra in different solvents: (A) water, (B) ethanediol, (C) ethanol, (D) DMF, and (E) acetone., along with corresponding optical band-gap (F, G, H, I, J). The QY values (K) and the decay curves (L) of R-CDs dispersed in different solvents (0.03 mg/mL).

(A) (D) (G) PL spectra of R-CDs in different solvents (ethanol, methanol, acetonitrile) with different water contents. (B) (E) (H) The linear relationship of I/I0 to water content and (C) (F) (I) the corresponding fluorescence photographs of representative samples.

(A) (D) (G) PL spectra of R-CDs in different solvents (isoproponal, DMSO, acetone) with different water contents. (B) (E) (H) The linear relationship of I/I 0 to water content and (C) (F) (I) the corresponding fluorescence photographs of representative samples.
Scheme illustration of R-CDs’ synthesis, property and application.
Luminescent parameters of R-CDs in different solvents.
The proposed method was applied to the determination of samples in water/ethanol system, and the values of three parallel experiments were found.

head.ce:title: Solvatochromism of high-efficient and long-wavelength emissive carbon dots for wide-range water-sensing in organic solvents

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel solvent-dependent fluorescent CDs (R-CDs) were prepared by solvothermal method from neutral red and urea precursors. The multicolor emissions ranging from blue to red from R-CDs could be achieved by tuning the solvents polarity. The highest quantum yield of 61.11% is achieved in R-CDs acetone solution with bright yellow emission at 565 nm. The solvent-dependent fluorescence is ascribed to the variation of CDs’ band-gap and surface potential, which are induced by polarity. R-CDs were used as a quantitative fluorescent sensor for detecting water in different solvents. An excellent linear relationship between water content (0–90%) and emission intensity is found in ethanol, methanol, acetonitrile, isopropanol, DMSO, and acetone. The limit of detection (LOD) is 1.55%, the recoveries range from 95% to 110%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) are 0.15%–5.43%. All results indicate that our R-CDs for water sensing exhibit good linear relationship and accuracy.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are luminescent nanoparticles consist of carbogenic cores and functional surfaces [1]. As one of the most remarkable carbon nanomaterials, CDs have generated great attention in recent years [2,3]. CDs as an excellent substitute for conventional fluorescent agents due to its metrics like high photostability, high quantum yield (QY), low toxicity, environment-friendliness, good photoluminescence (PL) property, low cost, and easy preparation [4–6]. For its excellent properties, CDs are extensively applied in bioimaging [7], sensing [8], and photocatalysis [9]. Compared with conventional CDs’ blue and green emission (400–530 nm), long-wavelength emissive CDs (λ em > 550 nm) have more advantages in applications like biological imaging and optical sensing, since the imaging quality could be significantly improved with larger penetration depth and reduced radiation damage [10,11]. Besides, until now, red/NIR-emitting CDs with high QY have been rare [12–14]. Therefore, exploring efficient long-wavelength-emitting CDs is highly desirable in further development.

Developing novel high-efficient and color-tunable CDs is considered as a significant strategy to expand the application fields. The fluorescence properties of CDs are easily affected by factors such as quantum size effects, surface states and radiation combinations, which can in turn impact the HOMO, LUMO and band gap of CDs. The conventional way to realize color-tunable CDs is using a fixed precursor while changing dopant and solvent under different temperature and reaction time, which is complicated and time-consuming [15]. Meanwhile, the solvent effect or solvatochromism, which depends on a strong interaction between CDs surface groups and organic solvent molecules, exerts an extremely powerful influence on CD optical properties when CDs are dispersed in various solvents [16–19]. It is a promising way to obtain color-tunable CDs fluorescence through solvatochromism.

It is well-known that in many cases, water is commonly present as an impurity in organic solvents. Water content control is crucial for drugs, chemicals, and food systems. Even a small amount of water can damage production equipment and lead to cause drug failure. Therefore, it is extremely important that water content could be detected visually and measured accurately. Conventional Karl-Fischer titration is a well-known method for measuring water content in organic solvents, requires expensive instruments, toxic reagents, precision and complex sample handling [20]. As an alternative, fluorescent sensors that utilize solvent-dependent luminescent variation have many advantages, such as low-cost, easy read-out and facile operation [21].

In this work, a novel red-emitting CDs (R-CDs) were easily synthesized using neutral red (NR) and urea as sources through a one-step hydrothermal treatment (Scheme 1 ). R-CDs show uniform size distribution as well as a non-excitation dependent emission phenomenon. Interestingly, by dispersing R-CDs in different polar solvents, color-tunable emission could be realized with peak wavelength shifting from 493 nm to 637 nm, covering the entire visible range. The QY values ranged from 11.31% to 61.11% in different solvents like water, ethanediol, ethanol, DMF, and acetone. In addition, it is found that the R-CDs emission intensity declines as the water content increases in various water/solvent systems. All results show that R-CDs could be a visual and quantitative fluorescent probe for determining water content in organic solvents.

NR and urea were brought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd and Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd, respectively. Ethanediol, ethanol, acetone, DMF, DMSO, isopropanol were purchased from Guangzhou GHTECH Co. Ltd. In a typical synthesis process, 0.0435 g NR and 0.0903 g urea were first added in 30 mL deionized water dissolved and followed by sonicating for 5 min. Afterwards, the solution was transferred into a hydrothermal reactor and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. The reddish-brown solution obtained was filtered using a microporous membrane (0.22 μm). Then, the pure product was obtained by drying for 12 h.

The R-CDs solution preparations for different testing are listed as followed.
1.
For pH stability test, 200 μL 0.03 mg/ml R-CDs water solution was mixed with 2800 μL buffer solution (pH = 2.89, 3.43, 4.47, 5.56, 6.5, 7.4, 8.2, 9.09, 10.15 and 11.92).
2.
For metal ions stability test, R-CDs powder (0.1 mg) was dispersed in water (550 μL), then R-CDs solution was mixed with 2050 μL PBS buffer solution and 400 μL metal ion solution (200 μmol/L). The concentration of R-CD in final solution was 0.03 mg/mL.
3.
For temperature stability test, R-CDs powder (0.1 mg) was dispersed in water (3 mL) to prepare the corresponding solution of 0.03 mg/mL

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken by the microscope (Tecnai F20). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on Thermo Scientific (ESCALAB 250Xi). UV–visible absorption spectra and Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured by a spectrophotometer (Metash UV-5200) and infrared spectrometer (Bruker/TENSOR II), respectively. Fluorescence spectra and lifetime decay were measured with a Edinburgh FLS980 spectrometer. Water content measurements were conducted using the Karl-Fischer Moisture Analyzer ADP513.

Details of lifetime measurements have been reported in our previous works [22,23]. The QY of R-CDs were examined using the FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer connected to the integrating sphere. First of all, R-CDs solutions (0.03 mg/mL) with different solvents were prepared. Then the corresponding emission spectra were collected, respectively. Finally, according to the photoluminescence measurement, each QY was calculated by using fluorescence software.

In a typical detection process, R-CDs powder (0.1 mg) was dispersed in certain solvent (3 mL), ethanol for example, to prepare the corresponding solution of 0.03 mg/mL. This solution served as a blank sample, and the corresponding PL intensity was labeled as I 0 (λ ex = 470 nm). Afterwards, constant volume of R-CDs solution (200 μL) was added into 2.8 mL of mixture (ethanol + water) with the changing water content of 0–90%. Then, the emission intensity (I) of each sample was measured using the same measurement parameters. Six different solvent-water mixtures (ethanol, methanol, acetonitrile, isopropanol, DMSO, acetone) with different water content were tested.

The LOD was determined as 4.408 μM by applying the formula LOD = 3σ/S, where σ denoted the standard deviation of the low concentration sample and S represented the slope. The reliability of the developed sensing method was evaluated using error bars representing the relative standard deviation (RSD) values.

As seen in TEM image (Fig. 1 A). The R-CDs are evenly distributed with a size range of 3–7 nm, and the average size is 5.3 nm. The high-resolution TEM in Fig. 1B and C confirms that R-CDs have a highly crystalline structure. A clear lattice fringe with a d-spacing of 0.22 nm corresponds to plane (100) of graphite. These results show that R-CDs have uniform size distribution and good dispersion. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1D) shows that several peaks at 3672, 3340, 2976 and 2894 cm−1, which could be attributed to the existence of O–H, N–H, –CC–H and C–H bonds. The peak values near 1594 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1 indicate the presence of –NO2/-NN- bonds and –CH3/-NO2 bonds. C–Cl stretching vibrations peak is located at 1063 cm−1 in FTIR spectrum. The Ar (σC-H) key position is 868 cm−1. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. 1E) shows four signals at 196.7, 284.0, 398.4 and 581.4 eV, confirming that R-CDs contain Cl, C, N, and O elements. In the high-resolution XPS of Cl 1s (Fig. 1F), two peaks at 196.78 and 198.38 eV could be attributed to C–Cl and N–Cl, respectively. In case of C 1 s (Fig. 1G), three distinct peaks at 283.88, 284.88 and 288.08 eV represent C–C, C–O, and CO, respectively. In case of N 1s (Fig. 1H), three peaks at 398.38, 400.18 and 405.68 eV are due to pyridine N, C–N and N–N, respectively. Fig. 1I (O 1s spectrum) shows two peaks at 531.28 and 532.18 eV from CO and C–O. Based on the FTIR and XPS analysis of R-CDs, it is suggested that the interaction between free Cl in neutral red and urea results in the generation of C–Cl. Meanwhile, the oxidation of NH2 in urea leads to the formation of –NO2. Besides, The FTIR and XPS analyses indicate that the R-CDs are comprised of aromatic structures and are surface-modified with numerous hydrophilic groups, such as –CO, –OH and –NO, which not only serve as the foundation for the red fluorescent emission, but also impart desirable characteristics such as water solubility and biocompatibility.

In order to study its optical properties, R-CDs ethanediol solution was chosen as a representative and the corresponding absorption, excitation and emission spectra were measured. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the UV–vis spectrum exhibits major absorption at 458 nm from n–π* transition. According to PLE spectrum, the optimal excitation wavelength (λ ex) is found as 470 nm. R-CDs show a single emission peak at 610 nm with FWHM of 130 nm. As can be seen from Fig. 2B, R-CDs have no excitation dependent. As the λ ex changes from 410 to 530 nm, the emission peak remains at 610 nm with changing emission intensity, in which the optimized excitation wavelength locates at 470 nm. The result confirms that only one kind of emission center exists.

The luminescent stabilities of R-CDs at different conditions, such as at metal ions, pH, temperature, are also studied. As shown in Fig. 2C, when the pH rises from 5 to 9, R-CDs emission intensity increases continuously. Later, the luminescence slightly quenches when R-CDs are in strong alkaline environment (pH > 9). It is obvious that the R-CDs show the lowest emission intensity at pH = 3 while the strongest one at pH = 9. It might be ascribed to the fact that surface functional groups of R-CDs were prone to hydrolysis in strong acid and alkaline environment, leading to the change of the fluorescence intensity [24]. Fig. S1 shows the emission wavelength almost does not change significantly with changing pH value. Besides, no obvious change in R-CDs emission intensity is observed (Fig. 2D), which proves that R-CDs have good ion stability and keeps its fluorescence in complex environment. Finally, the emission change at various temperatures was measured (Fig. S2). An increase of emission intensity is observed when temperature rises from 20 °C to 60 °C. The relationship between relative emission intensity (I/I
0 ) and temperature (T) could be described by linear equation: I/I
0 = 0.0123T + 0.267 (Fig. S3). The fluorescence intensity of R-CDs shows an excellent linear relationship (R
2 = 0.992), which indicates it could act as luminescent nanothermometer [25–27].

Interestingly, R-CDs have a fascinating trait that its emission color could be tuned when R-CDs are dispersed in different solvents with diverse polarity. After R-CDs are separately dissolved in n-hexane, acetone, DMF, ethanol, ethanediol and water, the maximum emission wavelength are 493 nm, 565 nm, 580 nm, 602 nm, 624 nm, and 637 nm, respectively (Fig. 3 and Fig. S4). As solvent polarity increases, the emitting color of R-CDs changes from blue to red at 470 nm excitation. Photos of R-CDs solutions with different solvent polarities at 470 nm excitation are shown in Fig. 3E. In order to study the polarity-induced redshift phenomenon, the corresponding absorption spectra were measured and shown in Fig. 3B, when the solvent polarity increases, the absorption wavelength also exhibits a red-shift, reveals that the band gaps (E g) of R-CDs could be modulated by solvent polarity. Absorption redshifts are usually associated with band-gap shrinkage and could be usually found in long-wavelength emission CDs [15]. The discoloration produced by R-CDs in different solvents could be described using the relative solvent polarity parameter (E
N
T ). The value of E
N
T represents both nonspecific (e.g., dipolar) and specific (hydrogen bonds; HB) the total solvability of solute-solvent interactions [28]. As seen from Table 1 and Fig. 3C, the emission wavelength has quite a good linear relationship with E
N
T . Fig. 3D depicts the possible scheme of energy levels variation with the changing solvent polarity, i.e., the emission redshift of R-CDs could be ascribed to the change of its surface electron states [29]. In protic solvents (e.g., ethanol, ethanediol, water), the R-CDs and solvent interact by hydrogen bonds (HB), effectively increasing the carrier density and resulting in a gradual energetic downward shift in energy in excited levels [30]. In aprotic solvents (e.g., n-hexane, acetone, DMF), electrons stabilize themselves through changing the dipole moment of the organic solvent molecules. When solvent polarity increases, the excited state charges transfer more electrons from the functional group to the surrounding solvent molecules, and the excited electrons lose more energy in the high polarity solvent than in the low polarity solvent, leading to the emission spectrum redshift [31].

The redshift phenomenon by the solvent polarity was further investigated by measuring both steady-state and dynamic spectra. As shown in Fig. 4 A–E, it is clear that the PL peak is irrelevant to the change of excitation wavelength. These results verify the existence of uniform emitting center of R-CDs, which excited states are solvent-sensitive and resulted in solvent-induced both absorption and emission red-shift. As can be seen in Fig. S5, photoexcitation greatly enhances the HB acceptance of R-CDs, possibly because the negative charge which located on the surface of the HB-accepting groups increased [32]. The observation of reduced charge density offers compelling proof of heightened electron cloud density in solvents of high polarity, thus facilitating long-wavelength emission [33]. It is further verify that the narrowing of the band gap is caused by the exposure of the surface states to different solvents [30]. As shown in Fig. 4F–J, the band gaps exhibit a gradually shrink with increasing solvent polarity. Fig. 4K demonstrates the QY of various R-CDs solutions, in which the highest QY is found as 61.11% in acetone solution. Fig. 4L shows the lifetime of various R-CDs solutions. The effective lifetime shows a declining tendency from 6.17 ns to 2.24 ns along with the emission quenching and peak red-shift, which is assigned to the competition between resonance energy transfer and radiative transition and leads to the shortened lifetime. This also indicates that the color-tuning phenomenon of R-CDs could be attributed to different surface excited states induced by different solvents. Besides, by using the equations: K
R = QY/τ, QY = K
R /(K
R + K
NR ), the calculation results show that the nonradiative (K
NR ) rates in water, ethanediol, ethanol, DMF, and acetone are 3.75 × 108 s−1, 2.07 × 108 s−1, 1.54 × 108 s−1, 9.90 × 107 s−1, and 6.30 × 107 s−1, respectively. It is obvious that the K
NR increases with the absorption and emission red-shift [34].

Due to above-mentioned solvatochromism property, R-CDs were applied in determining water content in organic solvents. Fig. 5 A–C shows the emission spectra and fluorescence color changes of R-CDs in a mixture of ethanol and water at an excitation wavelength of 470 nm, indicating an excellent linear relationship between water content (0–90%) and emission intensity. As seen in Table 2 , the LOD is 1.55%, the recoveries are between 95% and 110%, and the relative standard deviations RSD are 0.15%–5.43%. A comparison was made between the Karl Fischer method, which is widely used for determination of water, and a new fluorescence method to verify its accuracy. The experiments were conducted using ethanol as the medium, and both methods were employed to measure the water content in real ethanol samples. The results obtained from each method are presented in Table S1.

Inspired by this phenomenon, the change of fluorescence intensity in methanol, acetonitrile, isopropanol, DMSO, and acetone were measured. Figs. 5 and 6 display the correlation between the emission intensity and water content in different solvents, while Fig. S6 depicts the linear correlation between I/I
0 and water content. Moreover, Tables S2–S6 exhibit the recoveries and RSD of R-CDs in these solutions. Emission wavelength exhibits red-shift and the corresponding intensity decreases when the water content increases.

The decrease of fluorescence might be due to the fact that water disrupt the fluorescent ionic structure in R-CDs, leading to surface hydrolysis of the deprotic carboxyl group on R-CDs. Excited protons are difficult to transfer from the surface carboxyl group of CDs to the free ion pair for the complexation with water. Therefore, with rising water amount, the quenching of luminescence increases with the probability of proton transfer [35]. In order to verify the mechanism above, the lifetime of R-CDs were measured in ethanol solvent at different water contents (Fig. S7). It is found that the lifetime of R-CDs decreases with increase of water content, further confirms the acceleration of the rate of non-radiative transitions. The emission red-shift could be assigned to the energetic change of the redirected molecular dipole, and the excited electrons are lost to the higher polarity solvent [36]. The zeta potential of R-CDs at various water content levels were also measured (Fig. S8). The decreased charge density observed provides convincing evidence for the increased electron cloud density in solvents with high polarity, which promotes the emission of long-wavelength.

In summary, high-efficient (QY = 61.11%) and color-tunable (λ em = 493–637 nm) CDs were prepared by solvothermal method. This R-CDs exhibit a color-tunable phenomenon with changing solvent polarity. The emission peaks are 493, 565, 580, 602, 624 and 637 nm in n-hexane, acetone, DMF, ethanol, ethanediol, and water, respectively. Solvent-dependent fluorescence is ascribed to different surface excitation states of R-CDs induced by different solvents. With increasing solvent polarity, the surface of R-CDs can accept the negative charge of HB groups, increasing the electron cloud density, resulting in redshift and quenching of the R-CDs. By using solvatochromism phenomenon, the water content could be determined accurately by R-CDs in different organic solvents, especially in ethanol. The LOD is 1.55% and RSD is 0.15%–5.43% in broad detection range (0–90%). All results indicate that R-CDs is a promising fluorescent probe for water detection in different organic solvents.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research is funded by the Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2022GXNSFDA080005), Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. ZR2022ME113), Guangzhou Scientific planning program (No. 201804010260), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601081).
